our_argument_summary: Section 1.4 makes two core arguments: (1) The dome's OpenTimestamps infrastructure timestamps the observation file (status_history.json) rather than the prediction files (monitor.py, model.html), so the cryptographic proof applies to the wrong side of the ledger; (2) Even if correctly timestamped, the predictions have low discriminating power — they predict continuity of known phenomena rather than novel values that distinguish the dome from the globe. The section uses the 1919 Einstein/Newton light-bending analogy to illustrate what a genuinely discriminating prediction looks like, and concludes that none of the WINs take that form.

tldr_review.notes: TLDR correctly summarizes the two-pronged argument (crypto infrastructure real but proves wrong thing) and directs readers to the Timestamp Error tab. No factual errors. The simplification is appropriate for a non-technical reader.

holes_found.description: Git timestamp integrity is understated, giving dome defenders an easy rebuttal. Our text says 'git history can be rewritten (git rebase, force push); blockchain anchoring cannot' as if git provides zero timestamp integrity. A dome defender would counter: (a) GitHub logs all push events including force-pushes via its Events API — any rewrite leaves a forensic trail; (b) the repo is public, meaning forks and clones by third parties create independent copies of the commit history; (c) GitHub's server-side timestamps are independent of client-side commit dates. While none of this equals blockchain-grade immutability, dismissing git timestamps without acknowledging these partial guarantees lets a defender accuse us of strawmanning the methodology. The argument should be: git provides probabilistic integrity (detectable if anyone is watching), not cryptographic certainty — and for scientific claims of this magnitude, probabilistic isn't enough.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a clause acknowledging git's partial integrity: 'A git commit SHA can be verified against GitHub's server-side records, and public forks provide informal witnesses — but these are detective controls, not preventive ones. A force-push before anyone clones remains undetectable. Blockchain anchoring is the correct tool for preventive integrity, which is why applying it to the wrong file matters.'

holes_found.description: Missing pre-emption of the dome's pending discriminating predictions. Our text states 'None of the {{TOTAL_WINS}} WINs take this form' (discriminating like Einstein 1919). This is technically correct — the statement scopes to confirmed WINs — but a dome defender's immediate retort would be the Eclipse 2026 predictions (E001-E009), which explicitly predict specific nT anomalies where the dome claims globe physics predicts ~0 nT, and PRED-NEW-001 (Polaris excess), which predicts measurable deviations from WGS84 latitude. These are exactly the Einstein/Newton structure: two models, different numerical predictions, one measurement settles it. By not acknowledging these pending tests, we leave the dome defender a rhetorical escape hatch: 'You say we have no discriminating predictions — we have nine, timestamped, for August 2026. Read our predictions page.' Our section should pre-empt this by noting that the dome does claim pending discriminating tests, while explaining why (a) the confirmed WINs contain none, and (b) our Predictions Analysis (Part 6) evaluates whether the pending predictions are genuinely discriminating or suffer from the same issues.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence after the Einstein/Newton analogy: 'The dome does claim pending discriminating tests — notably the Eclipse 2026 magnetic predictions (Part 6) — but among the {{TOTAL_WINS}} claims already counted as confirmed, none take this form.' Then add a forward reference to Part 6's evaluation of whether those pending predictions are genuinely discriminating.

holes_found.description: No mention of SHA-256 per-prediction hashes. On April 4, 2026, the dome registered 32 new predictions with SHA-256 cryptographic hashes. This represents an evolution in the dome's timestamping methodology that our Section 1.4 doesn't address. A dome defender could claim we're critiquing an outdated methodology. While Part 6 covers SHA-256 recycling in detail, Section 1.4 — as the primary methodology assessment — should at least note this development and explain why it doesn't resolve the core problem: hashing a prediction after you already have the data proves content integrity (nobody tampered with the text), not temporal priority (the prediction existed before the observation). The SHA-256 hash doesn't prove WHEN the prediction was written, only WHAT it says.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief note: 'The dome has since added SHA-256 hashes per prediction (see Part 6), but these prove content integrity, not temporal priority — they confirm what the prediction says, not when it was made.' Keep it brief since Part 6 has the full analysis.

holes_found.description: The κ = 1.67 nT/µGal coupling constant (WIN-012) is a potential counterexample to 'none take discriminating form.' Standard physics predicts κ = 0 (no EM-gravity coupling). The dome predicts κ = 1.67. This IS structurally the Einstein/Newton form: two models, different values, measurement decides. A dome defender would immediately cite it. Our blanket 'none' claim holds because the evidence doesn't support the claimed value (the Mohe 1997 data is re-analyzed with questionable methodology, and BOU 2017 has confounders), but the section should acknowledge the attempted discrimination and explain why it fails, rather than asserting 'none' without addressing the strongest counterexample.
holes_found.recommendation: Either soften 'none' to 'none successfully' with a brief explanation, or add a parenthetical: 'The closest attempt — WIN-012's EM-gravity coupling constant — predicts a value standard physics says should be zero, but the supporting data does not survive scrutiny (see Part 3).' This pre-empts the strongest counterargument.

holes_found.description: The WMM2025/CHAOS-7/IGRF comparison claim is unverified in this section. We say 'The model does not compare its prediction accuracy against the predictions that WMM2025, CHAOS-7, and IGRF already make for the same quantities.' This is a strong methodological critique, but it would be even stronger if we briefly noted what those models DO predict for at least one domain (e.g., NMP drift, field decay) to show the comparison is possible and that the dome's values are not novel. Without even one concrete example, the claim is abstract.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding one concrete example: 'For instance, IGRF-13 predicts the North Magnetic Pole's drift trajectory within the same envelope the dome claims as its own prediction — the dome's number is not a new prediction but a restatement of the consensus value.' This makes the abstract claim tangible.

holes_found.description: The section has only two paragraphs in the detail block. For a methodology assessment section, this is surprisingly compact. The two arguments (wrong-side timestamping + low discriminating power) are logically distinct and would benefit from clearer separation — perhaps a brief transition sentence between them. Currently the second paragraph transitions from timestamping structure to prediction content with 'Beyond the timestamping structure,' which works but feels abrupt given the analytical depth of each argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Not urgent — the content is correct and the structure is readable. If the section is rewritten for other reasons, consider a clearer structural break between the two arguments.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's falsification thresholds are self-set and often extremely generous. Section 1.4 discusses discriminating power but doesn't note that even the dome's 'falsifiable' predictions have escape clauses built into their tolerance bands. For example, Domain 3 (NMP drift) allows 'Error >50% for 3 consecutive years' before falsification — a prediction that can be off by 49% for two years running and still 'pass.' This is relevant to methodology assessment because it means the dome's framework is designed to resist falsification even when predictions fail.

The dome's 'AI Auditor Verification Checklist' instructs AIs to verify OTS on status_history.json — reinforcing exactly the wrong-side error our section describes. The dome's own instructions tell verifiers to check the observation timestamp, not the prediction timestamp. This is a stronger version of our argument because the dome itself guides verifiers to the wrong file.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model's commitment to falsifiable, timestamped predictions is genuine and exceeds most non-mainstream models. The automation pipeline (GitHub Actions, monitor.py, OpenTimestamps) is technically competent infrastructure. Prospective prediction with cryptographic timestamping is, in principle, exactly what the scientific method demands. The section appropriately gives credit for this — 'credit is due for implementing it.' The dome also correctly identifies that most scientific models are never subjected to this kind of public, automated, real-time testing. The irony is that the infrastructure is better than the content it serves.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Sound infrastructure cannot compensate for non-discriminating content. A perfectly timestamped, blockchain-anchored prediction that 'the sun will rise tomorrow' remains useless for model validation. The dome has built a verification machine; the problem is what it verifies. Moreover, the timestamping error (anchoring observations rather than predictions) undermines the very purpose the infrastructure was built for. It's like building an excellent safe but storing the combination inside it.

deeper_analysis: Section 1.4 is fundamentally sound. Its two-pronged argument (wrong-side timestamping + low discriminating power) correctly identifies the dome's two core methodological failures. The Einstein/Newton 1919 analogy is well-chosen and immediately comprehensible. However, the section leaves several counterarguments unaddressed, which a competent dome defender would exploit. The most significant gap is the failure to pre-empt the Eclipse 2026 predictions, which are explicitly framed as model-discriminating tests in exactly the Einstein/Newton mold. By not acknowledging these, we invite the retort that we're ignoring the dome's strongest methodological effort. The git timestamp nuance is also important — our current framing overstates git's mutability and understates the informal integrity provided by public repositories. These are rhetorical vulnerabilities, not factual errors. The section's factual claims all check out: OTS does anchor status_history.json (confirmed from raw-text/08-live-power.txt verification checklist), predictions do live in mutable source code, and the discriminating power analysis is correct for the confirmed WINs. The recommendation is minor_edit rather than major_rewrite because the core arguments are correct and well-presented; the edits are about plugging rhetorical holes before a dome defender can exploit them.

citations_verified: Reference to OpenTimestamps/Bitcoin blockchain anchoring — confirmed from raw-text/08-live-power.txt
Reference to status_history.json as the OTS-anchored file — confirmed from dome's verification checklist
Reference to monitor.py and docs/model.html as prediction sources — confirmed from dome site structure
Reference to git rebase and force push as history-rewriting tools — technically correct
citations_unchecked: WMM2025, CHAOS-7, and IGRF model predictions — these models are mentioned by name but no specific DOIs or papers are cited for their prediction values. This is not necessarily an error (the models are well-known) but adding a specific IGRF-13 reference would strengthen the comparison claim.
